1: A type: Job is introduced as 'blameless (tām) and upright (yāšār), one who feared God and turned away from evil' — the fourfold moral description establishing his character before all that follows. This description is the ground of the book's theodicy: Job suffers not because of sin but because of who he is. Christ is the ultimate blameless and upright one (Hebrews 7:26 'holy, blameless, undefiled, separated from sinners'), whose suffering is the definitive demonstration that righteousness does not guarantee exemption from pain.
2: The theme: Job's seven sons and three daughters represent the fullness of covenant blessing (the number seven as completeness). The covenant family that Job enjoys images the Father's relationship with the Son in glory — Christ possessed the full riches of divine fellowship before the Incarnation (John 17:5 'restore to me the glory I had with you before the world was created').
3: The theme: Job's vast possessions establish him as the greatest man of the East. The prologue is careful to note his greatness before the stripping begins, so the magnitude of the loss is legible. Christ's kenosis similarly presupposes pre-existent glory: 'though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor' (2 Corinthians 8:9) — the poverty is only meaningful against the backdrop of the wealth.
4: The theme: the sons' feasting together with their sisters images covenantal fellowship and familial joy. The heavenly feast that Christ promises — 'I tell you, many will come from the east and the west, and will take their places at the feast with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven' (Matthew 8:11) — is the eschatological fulfillment of this pattern of gathered family at table.
5: A type: Job's intercessory offering for each of his children — rising early, offering burnt offerings, saying 'perhaps my sons have sinned' — is the priestly intercession that the NT applies to Christ. Hebrews 7:25 'he always lives to intercede for them'; 1 John 2:1 'we have an advocate with the Father — Jesus Christ, the Righteous One.' Job intercedes as patriarch-priest; Christ intercedes as eternal high priest. The regular (kol hayyāmîm) pattern of Job's intercession prefigures the perpetual intercession of Christ.
6: A revelation of God: the heavenly council where the divine beings (bĕnê ʾĔlōhîm) present themselves before YHWH establishes the cosmic-legal setting for the entire book. This is the same council before which Christ ascends (Hebrews 9:24 'he entered heaven itself, now to appear for us in God's presence'). The drama of Job's suffering has its origin in the heavenly court; so does the drama of Christ's passion and vindication.
7: A revelation of God: 'the Adversary came among them... from roaming through the earth.' 1 Peter 5:8 applies this directly: 'your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion seeking someone to devour.' The Adversary's roaming establishes his domain (the earth) and his method (surveillance for opportunity). Christ enters this same domain ('the Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us,' John 1:14) — the Son invades the Adversary's territory.
8: A type: YHWH's commendation — 'Have you taken notice of my servant Job? There is no one like him on earth' — echoes the divine commendation of the Son: 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased' (Matthew 3:17; Job 17:5). Job is YHWH's servant (ʿeḇeḏ) whose unique righteousness is publicly testified by God himself. Isaiah's Servant Songs use the same ʿeḇeḏ vocabulary for the figure who will bear the sins of many (Isaiah 42:1; Job 52:13).
9: A revelation of God: 'Does Job fear God for no reason?' — the Adversary's challenge cuts to the heart of all human piety: is it possible to worship God disinterestedly, without expectation of reward? This is also the question about Christ's disciples: will they remain faithful without earthly benefit? The book's resolution (Job maintaining integrity after losing everything) is the OT answer; the NT answer is the cross — Christ maintains faithfulness unto death, the ultimate disinterested love.
10: The theme: 'Have you not put a protective fence (śĕyāg) around him?' — the divine hedge as protection. The same concept appears in Christ's prayer: John 17:11-12 'Holy Father, protect them by the power of your name... I protected them and kept them safe.' For Job, this hedge is temporarily removed for testing; for Christ's people, the hedge of intercession is permanent.
11: A revelation of God: 'stretch out your hand and strike everything he has — he will surely curse you to your face.' The Adversary wagers that suffering will destroy Job's faithfulness. The same wager was made about Christ at Calvary: that the extremity of suffering and abandonment would produce curse rather than trust. The cross answers both wagers: Job does not curse; Christ speaks forgiveness (Luke 23:34).
12: A type: 'Very well, everything he has is in your power. Only do not lay a hand on the man himself.' YHWH limits the Adversary's access to Job, permitting loss but not his person. This is the precise inverse of what happens with Christ: the Father gives the Son entirely over — Romans 8:32 'he who did not spare his own Son, but gave him up for us all.' Job's person is protected; Christ's person is surrendered. The comparison illuminates the greater depth of Christ's sacrifice.
13: The theme: the day of feasting when disaster strikes — the enjoyment of covenant blessing at the moment of its catastrophic removal. The Last Supper has this quality: the night of fellowship and covenant when the betrayal, arrest, and passion begin (Matthew 26:20-30). The feast before the stripping.
14: The theme: the first messenger's report — the oxen and donkeys seized. The sequential stripping of Job's possessions by successive reports is the prologue's technique of narrating total loss. The progressive denudation mirrors the Passion sequence, where Jesus is stripped of clothing (John 19:23-24), dignity (the mocking, Matthew 27:28-31), and life.
15: The theme: 'I alone escaped to tell you' — the sole survivor formula repeated four times. The solitary witness who escapes to report bears the weight of the news alone. Christ's disciples fled (Mark 14:50 'everyone deserted him and fled'); the women at the tomb became solitary witnesses to the resurrection (Mark 16:1-8; Luke 24:1-10).
16: The theme: 'fire from God (ʾĕš ʾĔlōhîm) fell from heaven' — the second disaster invokes divine agency explicitly: not merely human enemies but apparently divine fire. Job experiences divine action as catastrophic. The NT reframes divine fire: Hebrews 12:29 'our God is a consuming fire' — but for those in Christ, this same fire is purifying rather than destroying (1 Peter 1:7).
17: The theme: the Chaldeans take the camels in the third disaster. The pattern of multi-front assault — enemies from multiple directions — prefigures the Passion's multi-front attack on Christ: Judas betraying, priests accusing, Roman soldiers executing, crowds mocking. Every human agent participates in the stripping.
18: The theme: the fourth and final disaster strikes the children themselves — the dearest possessions last. The pattern of progressive loss until the most precious is taken is also the Passion's movement: health taken, reputation taken, disciples taken, life taken. 'He who did not spare his own Son' (Romans 8:32) — the ultimate non-sparing.
19: The theme: 'a great wind (rûaḥ gĕḏôlāh) came out of the wilderness and struck the four corners of the house.' The wind that destroys Job's household uses the same vocabulary (rûaḥ = wind/spirit; gāḏôl = great) as the divine power. The wilderness origin of the destroying wind connects to the wilderness where Jesus was tempted (Matthew 4:1-11) and where the Adversary operates.
20: A type: Job's threefold mourning response — tearing his garment, shaving his head, falling to the ground in worship — is the ancient mourning liturgy (Genesis 37:34 Jacob; 2 Samuel 1:11 David). The fall to the ground in worship distinguishes Job's response from mere lamentation: grief and trust together. Christ's Gethsemane parallels this — 'he fell with his face to the ground and prayed' (Matthew 26:39), the physical prostration of utter surrender.
21: A type: 'Naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked I will return. The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away; may the name of the LORD be praised.' This is the theological high point of the prologue — the archetypal response to total loss. Paul develops the same logic: Philippians 4:11-12 'I have learned to be content whatever the circumstances'; 1 Timothy 6:7 'we brought nothing into the world, and we can take nothing out of it.' Christ's kenosis is the ultimate expression of this naked coming and returning: he empties himself (Philippians 2:7) to become poor (2 Corinthians 8:9) so that we might be clothed (Revelation 3:18).
22: A type: 'Job did not sin or accuse God of wrongdoing (tiplāh = what is unseemly/wrong)' — the blameless response to catastrophe. 1 Peter 2:22-23 applies exactly this standard to Christ: 'he committed no sin, and no deceit was found in his mouth. When they hurled their insults at him, he did not retaliate; when he suffered, he made no threats. Instead, he entrusted himself to him who judges justly.' Job anticipates the pattern; Christ perfects it.
